entries.title: About the Course
entries.content:
This course includes the following topic(s): Music Appreciation, Hymns & Spirituals, Folk Songs, Sol-fa: Musical Games, Afternoon Listening

About Music Appreciation
This course aims to foster a love of music. Students begin by learning about and listening to common orchestral instruments. Then they spend the rest of the course with a few famous pieces of music that serve as a fun introduction for students with no prior exposure to classical music.

About Hymns & Spirituals
Students sing hymns and spirituals to build their faith and appreciation of God's work across time. Hymns and spirituals are connected to the historical time period as appropriate.

About Folk Songs
Students sing folk songs connected to the time period (1800-1900).

About Sol-fa: Musical Games
Students play musical games to notice rhythm and melody.

Hymns & Spirituals
The Christian hymn and spiritual tradition is incredibly rich, and it is vanishing from many churches. In addition to bringing a much-needed moment of peace to your day, hymns can also introduce students to important theological ideas and church liturgy.

Folk Songs
The purpose of learning folk songs is twofold: to increase students’ enjoyment of music and to situate folk music in its historical context in an effort to give students a richer understanding of the times and places that birthed the songs. A musical score is sometimes included for songs, but does not always exactly match the audio used, since folk songs are primarily oral songs and often take many different forms. Studying folk music helps students grow deep roots in our shared heritage and gives insight into historical events from the perspective of the people who were there.

blocks.text:
• Connect each lesson to the previous one for that topic. 

• For music appreciation study, a recap might look like a brief discussion about the name or sound of the piece or instrument you listened to previously. 

• For hymns and folk songs, this might be an effort to hum the melody or recite the words of the song you're currently learning.

Prepare to listen to the day's musical selection or activity by inviting students to consider an interesting musical or historical idea in the lesson, perhaps a unique word, rhythm, instrument, or piece of historical background (usually included in lesson plans).

• Listen to the day's musical selection or practice the activity. 

• Music Appreciation lessons will usually have you listen to a piece of music for 3-8 minutes. If students have a difficult time listening for the duration of the lesson, stop the music at their limit.

• Movement is always a great way to encourage students to engage with the music. Sometimes the lessons will provide a suggestion, but students may make up their own using their whole bodies or creating finger plays. They may also draw, color, or act out a dance with stuffed animals, dolls, or other figures.

• Hymn, folk song, or musical games will sometimes have you listen to a part of the song and then practice echoing back the melody and/or words.
Let students share any thoughts or connections they have about the lesson.

lessons.title: 10m Hymns & Spirituals - Lesson 1
lessons.body:
How Firm a Foundation
⍞ Materials: Our Hymns, Our Heritage, Our Work Book, and/or Music Selections Booklet

➜ INTRO
Our first hymn this year is called "How Firm a Foundation." What is a foundation, and what do you think this hymn will be about?

➜ LISTEN & PRACTICE
Today, focus on getting familiar with the hymn. Read the words aloud and listen to the full song. Note that various recordings sometimes use the older "thee" rather than "you" and annotate your lyrics depending on the recording you use. Notice, talk about, and look up any unfamiliar words.
∞ Audio Link: How Firm a Foundation (YouTube or Spotify)

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Learn the history of the song from Our Hymns, Our Heritage.
"Text and Tune" p.136
lessons.teacherNotes:
• EXTRA HELPINGS
Look at early copies of Rippon's hymnal. What differences and similarities do you notice between it and hymnals today? If interested, read the original hymn (note the extra verses which are less common today) and the prefaces.
∞ Book Links: 1801 and 1828 versions

• AFTERNOON LISTENING
Use your preferred recording to listen to the hymn in the afternoons, evenings, or anytime! The more you spend time with it, the more the words will sink into your head and your heart.

• IMPORTANT DATE
"How Firm a Foundation" published in Rippon's hymnal (1787 AD).

lessons.title: 10m Folk Songs - Lesson 1
lessons.body:
Yankee Doodle
⍞ Materials: Our Work or Music PDF

PREP: Lyrics are included in your Our Work booklet or Music PDF. Print off the tune if you like as well.
∞ PDF Link: Yankee Doodle Sheet Music

➜ INTRO
What does the word "Yankee" mean? Our first folk song of the year is one of the most famous American folk songs, but it actually started as a British insult! Learn more about the history and then listen to the song today.
∞ Article Link: Yankee Doodle History

➜ LISTEN & DISCUSS
∞ Video Link: Yankee Doodle
lessons.teacherNotes:
★ TEACHER NOTE
Feel free to choose your own version of Yankee Doodle to learn. Just know that the lyrics vary widely, so another audio or video is unlikely to match the words in your Our Work or Music books. 

• AFTERNOON LISTENING
Be sure to listen to the term's songs throughout the week!

• IMPORTANT DATE
Yankee Doodle was first composed (~1755 AD)

lessons.title: 10m Hymns & Spirituals - Lesson 2
lessons.body:
How Firm a Foundation (cont.)
⍞ Materials: Our Hymns, Our Heritage and/or Music PDF or Our Work Book, Bible

➜ RECAP
What is the name of the hymn for this term, and what is it about? Tell anything else you remember about its history.

➜ SING
Practice the tune by first listening to a verse, then humming the next two.
∞ Video Link: How Firm a Foundation Full Song

➜ READ & DISCUSS
Let's focus on the lyrics a bit more. Read the whole song aloud, then look up the Bible verses below (mentioned last week in your hymnal) and tell what you notice about the images, ideas, or even direct words that the hymn writer used from each verse. 

- Isaiah 41:10 (Stanza 2)
- Isaiah 43:2 (Stanza 3 & 4)
- Zechariah 13:9 (Stanza 4)
- John 10:27-29, Romans 8:38-39, Hebrews 13:5-6 (Stanza 5)

➜ (Optional) COPYWORK (Grades 4+)
Write one of the Bible verses above underneath your hymn lyrics or in your copywork book.
lessons.teacherNotes:
★ TEACHER TIP
If teaching in a classroom setting, assign verses to various students to keep this part of the lesson from taking too long.

• EXTRA HELPING
More on the song's history and how the author used bible verses in his composition. Note the additional verse 
∞ Video Link: Hymn Story, Analysis, Performance

lessons.title: 10m Folk Songs - Lesson 2
lessons.body:
Yankee Doodle (cont.)
⍞ Materials: Our Work or Music PDF, United States Atlas or a Globe
 
➜ RECAP 
What do you remember about the history of "Yankee Doodle"?

➜ SING 
Listen to the full song, paying attention to the lyrics. Then listen again and hum along. You might want to march as well! Spend some time reading and memorizing the lyrics (use your Our Work or Music booklet).  
∞ Video Link: Yankee Doodle

➜ EXPLORE
With the remaining time or in the afternoon, explore pieces that have Yankee Doodle incorporated into them! Just as books make references to earlier works, composers often make musical references to other songs. The more you know about music, the more musical references you will catch! It's like picking up on an inside joke in a conversation!
∞ Article & Video: 200 Years of Yankee Doodle

lessons.title: 10m Hymns & Spirituals - Lesson 3
lessons.body:
How Firm a Foundation (cont.)
⍞ Materials: Our Hymns, Our Heritage and/or Music PDF or Our Work Book
 
➜ RECAP
Sing the tune of How Firm a Foundation on "hoo" and read the words aloud. Pay attention to the meaning and punctuation as you read.

➜ SING  & DISCUSS
Today, listen to a line of the song, then pause the video and echo it back, working your way line by line through the song. This will help you focus on each line of the song, not just the ones which come easily to you.
∞ Video Link: How Firm a Foundation Full Song

• What images or lines in the song do you like best? 

➜ COPYWORK (Grade 4+)
Copy a line of the melody into your music journal and (if you are taking sol-fa), see if you can label the notes with the sol-fa syllables. Hint: it is written in the key of G, and the first note is "So." Count up how many different sol-fa syllables you used in the song [there should be five]. This hymn is written using the pentatonic scale (often a major scale omitting fa and ti). Many other hymns (like Amazing Grace) are also written with only these five different sol-fa syllables. Explore Extra Helpings to explore a music theory rabbit hole on pentatonic music.

lessons.title: 10m Folk Songs - Lesson 3
lessons.body:
Yankee Doodle (cont.)
⍞ Materials: Our Work or Music PDF

➜ RECAP 
Can you hum the tune of "Yankee Doodle"? 

➜ SING & PRACTICE
Write down the first line of each verse to help yourself remember the order. Then sing along with the recording. If you need to focus more closely on the words, pause the video after a line and echo it back yourself.
∞ Video Link: Yankee Doodle

➜ WRITE (optional)
Like most folk songs (and even hymns), many different verses have come and gone from Yankee Doodle. If you want a challenge, try composing some of your own additional or alternate verses! Try singing your lyrics with the tune to make sure you have the right rhythm (you'll notice that there are four beats in each line).
lessons.teacherNotes:
• MUSIC CONNECTION
If you play an instrument, try playing the melody of Yankee Doodle!

lessons.title: 10m Hymns & Spirituals - Lesson 4
lessons.body:
How Firm a Foundation (cont.)
⍞ Materials: Our Hymns, Our Heritage and/or Music PDF or Our Work Book
✂︎ GATHER SUPPLIES: Paints or sketching materials

➜ RECAP
 Recite the hymn (as much as able).

➜ SING
 Try to sing the whole song with the accompaniment. Note the areas where you aren't as sure of the tune or the words.
∞ Video Link: How Firm a Foundation Tune

➜ ILLUSTRATE
With remaining time (and feel free to continue on your own time if desired), sketch or paint an illustration for each verse in your music notebook or alongside your lyrics.
lessons.teacherNotes:
★ TEACHER TIP
If a student has a hard time coming up with an idea for an illustration, encourage them to focus on just one image from a verse to illustrate. There are no wrong illustrations as long as the student connects it to an aspect of the verse in their mind. The purpose is simply to help them inhabit and reflect on the verse in a different way.

lessons.title: 10m Folk Songs - Lesson 4
lessons.body:
Yankee Doodle (cont.)
⍞ Materials: Our Work or Music PDF

➜ RECAP 
Sing as much of "Yankee Doodle" as you can from memory. Note the spots where you get stuck!

➜ SING & PRACTICE
Read the lyrics and work on any spots you got stuck. Then try singing the song with the instrumental (use your lyrics, not the video's). When it ends, keep singing the rest of the verses a cappella. Make up your own accompaniment by clapping or marching if you like!
∞ Video Link: Yankee Doodle Tune

➜ DISCUSS
Can you imagine being a soldier and first hearing this tune as an insult? Can you think of any other examples of something that started as a put-down but was then reclaimed to mean something else?
lessons.teacherNotes:
• EXTRA HELPINGS (Grade 10+)
Read Percival Everett's short story "The Appropriation of Cultures" (linked in Extra Helpings) to think more about how songs or other cultural symbols gain and change connotations.

lessons.title: 10m Sol-fa: Musical Games - Lesson 5
lessons.body:
Dwarfs and Giants

➜ LISTEN & PRACTICE
Listen to "In the Hall..." by Edvard Grieg. Tiptoe with hands on hips when the music is soft. When it is loud, pretend to be a giant and march with your hands high in the air. During the second song, stand in one place, but move your body up and down as the music gets louder or softer. You can also swing your arms and twist with the music. Ensure that larger groups or students with spatial perception differences have enough room.
∞ Audio Link: In the Hall of the Mountain King
∞ Audio Link: Morning Mood

lessons.title: 10m Hymns & Spirituals - Lesson 5
lessons.body:
How Firm a Foundation (cont.)
⍞ Materials: Our Hymns, Our Heritage and/or Music PDF or Our Work Book
 
➜ RECAP 
Sing hymn from memory (as able).

➜ SING 
Now that you know the words better, experiment with how you sing the song. Pick a few ideas to try or come up with your own. Think about how to emphasize the meaning of the words by the way you sing different verses. Are there other instruments you would use? Use the recording as needed.

• Sing some verses faster or slower.
• Sing some parts soft and some loud.
• Sing the song as a 2-part canon. To do this, divide into two groups (if with at least two people) or use the recording as your second person if solo. Group 1 should start singing, and then Group 2 begins at the beginning when Group 1 hits the "tion" in "foundation" in the first line (see #57 in Our Hymns, Our Heritage to see where Group 2 comes in). Because of the way the song is composed, the two groups will sound nice together even while singing different words! 

∞ Audio Link: How Firm a Foundation Tune
lessons.teacherNotes:
• EXTRA HELPINGS
Experiment with more than two groups singing this hymn in a canon or round, and try starting at different places to see what sounds best. For instance, Group 2 could wait to start until Group 1 begins the second line of music in your hymnal. Or you could try having four different groups!

lessons.title: 10m Folk Songs - Lesson 5
lessons.body:
Yankee Doodle (cont.)
⍞ Materials: Our Work or Music PDF

➜ RECAP 
Sing as much of Yankee Doodle as you can from memory.

➜ SING & PRACTICE
Work on any spots you struggled with in the recap. Then, using the instrumental track or just your voice, sing all of Yankee Doodle (use lyrics if needed). Experiment with how you sing each verse. Try singing one loud and proud, another like you're telling a secret to a friend, etc. If you wrote any of your own verses, add them in as well!
∞ Video Link: Yankee Doodle Tune

➜ WRITE (optional)
Refine or add more of your own verses to the song!

lessons.body:
Children of the Heavenly Father
⍞ Materials: Our Hymns, Our Heritage

(Optional) PREP: Read p.170 in your hymnal and the longer article about Lina and mark additional sections to share.
∞ Article Link: More about Lina

➜ RECAP

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
You might not have heard of our second hymn this term, but it is a beautiful reminder of God's love for his children. Read from the hymnal and share any additional sections from the article as desired (older students can read the article directly).  
Our Hymns, Our Heritage, "Text and Tune" p.170

➜ LISTEN, PRACTICE, & DISCUSS
Bookmark #74 in Our Hymns, Our Heritage, and read the English words aloud. Then listen to the full song.
∞ Video Link: Children of the Heavenly Father YouTube
∞ Audio Link: Children of the Heavenly Father Spotify

• What did you think of the song after hearing a bit about Lina's life?
• Discuss any unfamiliar words.
lessons.teacherNotes:
• IMPORTANT DATE

lessons.title: 10m Folk Songs - Lesson 6
lessons.body:
Sakura, Sakura
⍞ Materials: Our Work or Music PDF, Globe or World Atlas

➜ RECAP
Hum or sing the first few verses of Yankee Doodle.

➜ INTRO
Today we're starting a new folk song about cherry blossoms! The tune originated sometime during the Edo Period (1603-1868) in Japan, which was a time of relative stability and peace. Over the years, it has become internationally famous and has been performed around the world.

➜ VIEW & MAPWORK
Cherry blossoms have been important to Japanese culture for centuries, and festivals to view them in peak season take place every spring. Learn a bit more about this tradition and look at the pictures of some of the beautiful places in Japan. You may want to pull out Google Maps or an atlas and locate the different places mentioned as well!
∞ Article Link: Sakura in Japan

➜ LISTEN 
Listen to the song once or twice and follow along with the lyrics. Print off the sheet music tune if desired.
∞ Video Link: Sakura, Sakura
∞ Image Link: Sakura Sheet Music

lessons.body:
Skipping Part 2

➜ RECAP
Skip in a circle. If skipping is too challenging, students may shuffle instead.

➜ PRACTICE
We're going to skip more today, but this time, see if you can skip sideways! Try sideways skipping (left and right). (If there is a group of students, have them take hands and skip sideways in a ring.) Try to make up another way to skip (maybe backwards!). After you feel confident, skip and sing with the music.
∞ Audio Link: Skip to My Lou

lessons.title: 10m Hymns & Spirituals - Lesson 7
lessons.body:
Children of the Heavenly Father (cont.)
⍞ Materials: Our Hymns, Our Heritage and/or Our Work or Music PDF, Globe or World Atlas
 
➜ RECAP & MAPWORK
• What is "Children of the Heavenly Father" about?
• Locate Sweden on your atlas or globe. Can you find Småland or Stockholm?

➜ READ & SING
• Read the lyrics aloud. 
• Write down the beginning of each verse to help you remember the order. 
• Work on the tune by alternating between listening and humming with the verses. Slow down and pause the tune after each line and hum the melody back if you run into any tricky spots.
∞ Music Links: Children of the Heavenly Father (YouTube or Spotify)

➜ DISCUSS
Can you think of any Bible stories or verses that Lina might have thought of when she wrote this song? [One option to read and discuss: Isaiah 40:11 and see the Extra Helping link for additional reflections on each verse]
lessons.teacherNotes:
• EXTRA HELPINGS
∞ Article Link: Reflections on CoftHF

lessons.title: 10m Folk Songs - Lesson 7
lessons.body:
Sakura, Sakura (cont.)
⍞ Materials: Our Work or Music PDF

➜ RECAP 
Can you hum the beginning of the tune of Sakura, Sakura? 

➜ SING & PRACTICE
Listen to the song and pay attention to how each word is pronounced. Then listen again and pause the video after each line to echo it back. 
∞ Video Link: Sakura, Sakura

➜ VIEW
The song is often performed on the koto, a Japanese stringed instrument which became very popular in the 1700s. Listen to this instrumental version of our song with extra time.
∞ Video Link: Sakura Sakura (Koto)

lessons.title: 10m Music Appreciation - Lesson 7
lessons.body:

∞ Video Link: Children of the Heavenly Father Tune

➜ READ & DISCUSS
Tell what you know about events happening around the world in the 1800s from anything you've learned in other subjects. 

One of the reasons this hymn became so popular not only in Swedish but in English was due to two things: the famous soprano Jenny Lind who toured in North America and sang some of Lina's songs and the number of Swedes who immigrated to North America. We'll learn more about Jenny Lind next lesson.

lessons.body:
Sakura, Sakura (cont.)
⍞ Materials: Our Work or Music PDF

➜ RECAP

➜ LISTEN & SING 
Listen to the full song once, following along with the words. Then try to sing the song yourself a cappella (with just your voice).
∞ Video Link: Sakura, Sakura

➜ STUDY
If you take sol-fa or an instrument, look at the sheet music of this folk song and count how many different tones are used. The same note in a different octave doesn't add to the count (for instance, just count one B). How many different tones did you find? [Answer: 5] Much of Japanese music is composed with a scale of five notes, and this song uses the hirajoshi scale (see examples in the link below). This scale is a default scale for the koto instrument as standardized by a famous Koto player in the 17th century, Yatsuhashi Kengyō. Play around with these five notes and see what other tunes you can come up with, or try playing Sakura starting on a different note than shown in the sheet music, but using the same scale.
∞ Image Link: Sakura Sheet Music
∞ Article Link: Hirajoshi Scale
lessons.teacherNotes:

lessons.title: 10m Hymns & Spirituals - Lesson 14
lessons.body:
Angels We Have Heard On High (cont.)
⍞ Materials: Our Hymns, Our Heritage and/or Our Work or Music PDF

➜ RECAP 
What is "Angels We Have Heard On High" about?

➜ STUDY & SING 
Today, we're focusing on learning the tune and words separately. 
• Even if you can't read music, look at the music in your hymnal and notice that the shape of the top notes in the first line is the same as the second line. So once you learn the tune of the first line of music, you've learned the tune for the rest of the verse! 
• Now find the first "Deo" you see in the sheet music. Even though it looks like a lot of notes, you'll see in your Our Work book or Music PDF and notice in the music that for the chorus you sing "Gloria in excelsis Deo" twice. And the only note that is different the second time is the very last note of the song. 
• Now listen to the song and pay attention to those repeated lines (feel free to highlight them with different colors or make a note in your lyrics if desired)! Hum along with the recording as you pick up the tune.

∞ Video Link: Angels We Have Heard (YouTube or Spotify)

➜ READ & DISCUSS
Read the words of the song aloud either in your hymnal, Our Work, or Music pdf.
lessons.teacherNotes:
• MUSICAL NOTE
When you sing "Gloria" in the chorus, you are singing a melisma – many notes on one syllable. While this is common for songs featuring just one artist, it is uncommon for congregational songs. Can you think of any other melismas in songs you've sung? Melismas are sometimes called "runs" or "riffs" in pop music.

lessons.title: 10m Folk Songs - Lesson 18
lessons.body:
En roulant ma boule
⍞ Materials: Our Work or Music PDF, colored pencils, or markers

➜ RECAP
Sing Amazing Grace from memory (or look at lyrics if needed). Think about the words and try to sing in a way that matches them!

➜ INTRO
We're learning a new folk song today! In the 17th and 18th centuries, France claimed a significant portion of land in North America (see map link below). France's financial situation eventually collapsed, and they lost the Seven Years' War with England in 1763, but French voyageurs spent decades paddling through the Great Lakes and down the Mississippi and Missouri rivers (see Extra Helpings for more on the Voyageurs!) to transport furs. As they paddled, they sang to pass the time and keep their strokes even. Our folk song for the rest of this term was one of the songs the voyageurs sang.
∞ Map Link: New France in its Heyday

➜ STUDY
The story of this song is odd and looks long, but you'll notice why it's a good paddling song when you listen to the tune and rhythm. But first, look at the lyrics in your Our Work/Music book. With your colored pencils or markers, mark, highlight, or underline the lines which are repeated (for instance, use the same color to highlight/mark the second and fourth line of each verse, which are ALWAYS the same).

➜ LISTEN
If you can, follow along with the lyrics in your book as you listen. Pay attention especially to the refrain today and focus just on learning those words. 
∞ Video Link: En roulant ma boule
∞ Image Link: En roulant sheet music (optional; helpful if you want to look at the tune)

lessons.body:
O Worship the King (cont.)
⍞ Materials: Our Hymns, Our Heritage and/or Our Work or Music PDF, Bible
 
➜ RECAP 
Can you hum the melody of "O Worship the King"?

➜ SING
Sing along with the hymn today.
∞ Video Link: O Worship the King (YouTube V1, V2, or Spotify)

➜ READ & DISCUSS
• Sir Robert Grant was inspired by Psalm 104, or perhaps both Psalm 104 and a paraphrase of it from the 16th century by William Kethe (who wrote "All People That On Earth Do Dwell"). 
• Read Psalm 104:1-4 (or further if you like) and discuss the ways you see Grant working the ideas from the psalm into his poetry. Do you see echoes of any other Bible passages?
lessons.teacherNotes:
• EXTRA HELPINGS
The image of God riding upon the wind is common in both Scripture and in other hymns. If you learned "God Moves in a Mysterious Way" in Alveary hymn lessons last year, you might remember the line "he plants his footsteps in the sea and rides upon the storm." Look up Psalm 19, Isaiah 19, and Nahum 1 for other Bible passages with this imagery. Why do you think God is described this way?

lessons.title: 10m Folk Songs - Lesson 20
lessons.body:
En roulant ma boule
⍞ Materials: Our Work or Music PDF

➜ RECAP 
Tell what you remember about the people who often sang "En roulant ma boule" in the 1600s and 1700s.

➜ LISTEN & PRACTICE
As French voyageurs paddled, they often sang in a call-and-response format where the leader sang one line and the rest of the men responded. Try singing "En roulant ma boule" in this style by singing along on repeated sections. For instance, let the singer sing the first two lines of the refrain, then you join in when it's repeated. Similarly, listen to the first two lines of each verse and then try joining in when they are repeated. (The singer tries to naturally mimic this call-and-response style by singing louder on the repeated sections.)
∞ Video Link: En roulant ma boule

lessons.title: 10m Hymns & Spirituals - Lesson 30
lessons.body:
When Morning Gilds the Skies
⍞ Materials: Our Hymns, Our Heritage and/or Our Work or Music PDF

➜ RECAP 
Sing "Hallelujah! What a Savior."

➜ INTRO
• What was happening in the world during the 1800s? Tell about a few things you have learned in other subjects.
• Our last hymn for the year is from the mid-1800s. Don't forget that people have always sung these hymns in the midst of world events and the joys and struggles of everyday life – which you're learning about in other subjects!

➜ SING & PRACTICE
Read the lyrics aloud, discuss unfamiliar words, and then listen to the full song (Note that the first version skips verse 3 in your lyrics, and the second version includes a 5th verse. So decide whether you'd like to learn three or five verses and adjust your lyrics accordingly.)

∞ Music Links: When Morning Gilds the Skies (YouTube V1, V2, or Spotify)

➜ READ & DISCUSS
Learn the history of the song from your hymnal.
"Text & Tune" p.203
lessons.teacherNotes:
• IMPORTANT DATE
"When Morning Gilds the Skies" published (German: 1828 AD, English 1854 AD)

• TERMINOLOGY
-gild: to cover with a thin layer of gold
-canticle: song

Fun fact: The last quarter or so of the 19th century is sometimes called the "Gilded Age" in US history.

Read the lyrics aloud and pay close attention to the first line of each verse, as that is the orienting piece of each verse, since they all end with the same refrain.

➜ EXPLORE

terms.content:
Hymns & Spirituals
· Sing "Children of the Heavenly Father" and tell what you know about the life and times of Carolina Berg. 

Folk Songs
· Sing "Yankee Doodle" (add in your own verses if you came up with some!) OR "Sakura, Sakura" and tell a little about the song.

Sol-fa: Musical Games
· Sing a favorite song, do a favorite activity/dance, or draw/paint a favorite piece.

Hymns & Spirituals
· Sing "Angels We Have Heard on High" OR Draw or paint a new illustration to go with one of your hymns from this term. Underneath your work, write the line or verse from the hymn that your illustration reflects and write or tell why you chose the passage to illustrate.

Folk Songs
· Sing "Amazing Grace" and the refrain (or more if you want) of "En roulant ma boule."

Sol-fa: Musical Games
· Sing a favorite song, do a favorite activity/dance, or draw/paint a favorite piece.

Hymns & Spirituals
